More form differentiation (cascading vs upright vs nest-form) and features like Gentsch White's pale tips would close the gap.

summaries.summary: The fern archetype uses a radial frond-burst that reads acceptably as a fern from top-down, but distinctiveness is the core problem: 9 of 10 species are near-identical green starbursts varying only in size, and only japanese-painted-fern (correctly purple) stands out. Signature foliage colors are missed on autumn-fern (coppery new growth) and cinnamon-fern (brown central fertile fronds), and the size ranking looks somewhat inverted (holly-fern largest while genuinely bigger ferns render smaller). Only one top-down image exists for this archetype; no side or bloom views were available.
summaries.group_similarity: Not adequately distinct. Nine of the ten ferns (autumn, boston, christmas, cinnamon, holly, lady, maidenhair, ostrich, royal) are essentially interchangeable green radial starbursts separated only by size and density; only japanese-painted-fern is recognizably different, and only because of its purple color. Since ferns never bloom, all differentiation must come from foliage form and color, and the shared 'spiky burst' geometry plus uniform green delivers almost none. The single distinct case (Japanese painted fern's purple) shows the fix: give the signature-colored ferns their real foliage tint (autumn fern's copper new growth, cinnamon fern's brown fertile spikes) and vary crown density/spread more aggressively.

summaries.summary: The globe archetype's overall form (a floret-ball on a stem with strappy basal leaves) is a good, faithful match for agapanthus, allium, gomphrena, sea holly and chives across side and top-down views. The main gaps are distinctiveness: the blue agapanthus cultivars and the large purple alliums each collapse into near-identical spheres, plus a handful of scale/color oddities (Peter Pan dwarf rendered largest, Mount Everest large-white rendered tiny, Twister missing its bicolor eye). No form-wrong or unrecognizable-model issues.
summaries.group_similarity: Mixed. The gomphrena/globe-amaranth set is a clear positive: six cultivars cleanly separated by color (magenta, lavender, orange, purple, red 'Strawberry Fields', white). The two weak clusters are (1) the blue agapanthus cultivars, which are near-identical blue globes differing only in size/shade even though Twister should be a blue-eyed white and Midnight Blue/Storm Cloud far darker, and (2) the large purple alliums (giant allium, gladiator, globemaster, purple-sensation), interchangeable purple spheres that in reality vary greatly in head size and form. Distinct-color members (allium-moly yellow, allium-drumstick maroon, sea-holly steel-blue) read well.

summaries.summary: The bulb archetype is in good shape: the flower-on-a-stem-from-a-tuft form is correct and cultivar colors are impressively accurate in the April bloom captures (tulips, hyacinths, muscari, freesia, fritillaria and anemones all match their names; no clearly-wrong colors found). The main weakness is the default June view, where nearly every species is a dormant green foliage blob (only ~5 bloom in June), creating a big distinctiveness gap in the app's default month. Secondary issues are generic bloom shapes: oversized ball blooms on the small anemone/windflower plants, trumpet-less daffodils, hyacinth vs muscari both reduced to grape-cluster blobs, and windflower-* duplicating anemone-blanda-*.
summaries.group_similarity: Within the bloom month (April) distinctness is adequate but is carried almost entirely by color, not form: cultivars within a genus (tulips, hyacinths, muscari, freesias) are cleanly separated by accurate colors, which is legitimate since they differ mainly by color in real life too. Form does little identity work, so several convergences appear: hyacinth and grape hyacinth are the same grape-cluster blob; all narcissus are plain eggs with no trumpet (white daffodils are mutually indistinguishable and indistinguishable from other white bulbs); tulips, crocus and narcissus of a shared color all read as the same egg; and windflower-* is a literal duplicate of anemone-blanda-*. The biggest similarity problem is not in April but in the default June capture, where ~95% of the archetype collapses to identical green blobs because the bulbs are out of season.

summaries.summary: The daisy archetype (190 species across 7 June pages) is largely acceptable: cultivar colors are accurate and cultivar-appropriate throughout (verified against the September bloom sheets, where asters, mums, black-eyed susans and coneflowers all bloom in their correct hues). No wrong-color findings. The main weaknesses are distinctiveness: same-color members across different genera are near-interchangeable, structurally signature genera (echinacea cone, gerbera solitary blooms) lose their form, and the Profusion zinnias are scaled as the group's giants (backwards from reality).
summaries.group_similarity: Within-archetype distinctiveness is driven almost entirely by flower color and cluster size. Same-color members across unrelated genera (the white daisies; the many yellow/gold daisies like bidens, coreopsis, calendula-yellow, gazania-yellow, gerbera-yellow, chrysanthemum-yellow) are nearly indistinguishable, and structurally distinctive plants lose their signature silhouettes: coneflowers have no raised cone, gerbera are not solitary large blooms, cosmos lack their wispy foliage/8-petal form. This is inherent to one archetype spanning ~190 species and is an acceptable low-poly tradeoff, but it is a genuine gap - a gardener could not tell a coneflower from a gerbera from an aster of the same color. Overall the archetype reads correctly as daisy-family mounding clumps, colors are right, and phenology is handled well (fall bloomers correctly green in June, blooming in September).

summaries.summary: The foliage-clump archetype covers ~66 plants and, for true low-mounding perennials (hostas, Heucheras/coral-bells, rex begonias, bergenia, brunnera, lungwort, lamb's ear) the low-poly clump form and per-plant COLOR are largely accurate and impressively well-differentiated by hue. The one clear structural error is the 12 cannas, which are tall upright flowering tropicals wrongly rendered as flat ground-hugging mounds with no blooms. Secondary issue: within each hue band the clumps are interchangeable, so several large same-color clusters (dark-maroon, chartreuse, plain-green, blue-grey) need shape/height/variegation cues to distinguish very different plants.
summaries.group_similarity: Macro color diversity is good - the palette spans green, gold/chartreuse, blue-grey, maroon/purple, red, orange, pink, silver and white, and named cultivars mostly land on the right hue. The weakness is intra-hue: every model is the same rosette of overlapping leaf polygons with a few center spheres, so once two plants share a color they are effectively the same model (e.g. the dark-maroon group of Heucheras + Black Magic elephant ear + dark cannas + burgundy begonia; the chartreuse hosta/heuchera/canna group; the plain-green group). Height, leaf shape, and variegation carry no signal. The cannas are the outlier that most damages the archetype's honesty: they are forced into a low mound that erases their tall banana-leaf architecture and signature flower spikes. Adding form cues (upright silhouette for cannas, variegated margins for the striped hostas, a genuine silver tone for lamb's ear) would close most of the gap.

summaries.summary: The iris archetype (a fan-of-sword-leaves form) is correct for all members and the daylily cultivars are genuinely well color-differentiated and accurate. The weakness is June distinctiveness: all 11 gladiolus and all 6 crocosmia show no bloom and collapse into near-identical green sword fans, and no other-month images exist to rescue their identity. No wrong-color or form-wrong problems found.
summaries.group_similarity: Mixed. The daylily blooms are the strength of this batch - distinctly colored (orange, apricot, orchid-pink, multiple yellows, cream, red, purple, gold, pink) and easy to tell apart, and the three true irises read as purple/blue. The weakness is the two non-blooming-in-June groups: the 11 gladiolus cultivars are mutually indistinguishable green fans, as are the 6 crocosmia. These color-named cultivars need a differentiator to be adequately distinct in a June-only view.

summaries.summary: Groundcover archetype (55 species, top-down June) is mostly acceptable: a clear majority bloom in June and are distinguished well by flower-dot color, and several foliage-color specials (lamium, snow-in-summer, ann-folkard, the sweet-potato-vine set) are accurate. The main problem is the 11 creeping-phlox cultivars, which all render as an identical plain-green mound out of bloom and cannot be told apart. A secondary issue is a ghostly washed-out pale rendering on a few spring bloomers (basket-of-gold, rock-cress, epimedium) that makes them look dead - basket-of-gold in particular loses its defining golden bloom entirely.
summaries.group_similarity: The whole groundcover archetype shares one "cluster of low spheres" mesh, so species are distinguished only by flower-dot color and foliage tint. This is adequate for the majority that bloom in June (13 hardy geraniums, 7 portulaca, 6 sweet alyssum, thyme, ice-plant, periwinkle, the three roses, etc. all read distinct via flower color), and a few foliage-tint plants are genuinely well done: lamium and snow-in-summer correctly show silver foliage, ann-folkard shows correct chartreuse-leaf + magenta, and the sweet-potato-vine set spans lime/bronze/near-black-purple/white variegation nicely. The distinctiveness collapses only for spring bloomers shown out of flower - above all the 11 creeping-phlox cultivars, which become one indistinguishable green mound.

summaries.summary: The grass archetype reads correctly as clumped grass with mostly-accurate colors and no wrong/unrecognizable models, but distinctiveness is weak: with 33 species reduced to about five color families and near-identical radiating blade sprays, plants differ almost only by color. The two real identifiers - plume shape and plant scale - are largely missing (only 7 cool-season grasses show a shared tan seed-head at June, and giant pampas grass is rendered the same size as tiny blue fescue). Pink Feather Pampas loses its pink identity entirely.
summaries.group_similarity: Distinctiveness within the grass archetype collapses almost entirely to blade color. There are 33 species but effectively ~5 visual families (blue/glaucous, red/burgundy, gold/yellow, white/variegated, plain green), and within each family the models are near-identical radiating blade sprays. The two features that actually separate these grasses in reality - the inflorescence/plume shape and overall plant scale - are both largely absent: at June (month 6) only the 7 cool-season grasses show a (shared, identical) tan seed-head, and all plants render at roughly the same size, so a 20cm blue fescue is indistinguishable in footprint from a 3-4m pampas grass. Gold group (blue-fescue-gold, feather-reed-grass-gold, japanese-forest-grass-gold, maiden-grass-gold, pampas-grass-gold) and white/variegated group (feather-reed-grass-white, japanese-forest-grass-white, maiden-grass-white, northern-sea-oats-white) are similarly interchangeable. Adding size variation and month-appropriate plume shapes would do the most to make this archetype adequately distinct.

summaries.summary: The nodding-cup archetype (18 species) splits into 7 columbines and 11 hellebores. Columbines are correctly colored and well-differentiated by their June flowers, and the overall nodding-flower-on-stem form is right. The one real issue is a distinctiveness gap: all 11 hellebores appear as identical green blobs at the June capture (seasonally accurate but no bloom-month image exists to tell them apart), so most of the archetype is indistinguishable.
summaries.group_similarity: Mixed. The 7 columbines are well-differentiated: each shows correctly-colored nodding flowers in June (purple, dark maroon, blue, pink, red, white, yellow) and is easy to tell apart. The 11 hellebores are the opposite - all reduced to nearly identical green foliage sphere clusters with no bloom at June, so they are indistinguishable from one another and should be made more distinct (either by capturing their spring bloom, since no other-month image exists for this archetype, or by varying foliage form/tint). The nodding-cup 3D form itself (foliage mound plus flowers on thin nodding stems) reads correctly for both plant types in the side view.

summaries.summary: The lily archetype is really a broad "flower-on-a-stem" bucket dominated by hibiscus (~24) and amaryllis (8), with only ~5 true lilies. Colors on the blooming models are generally correct, but every bloom is the same generic multi-petal pom, so distinct real flower shapes (calla spathe, recurved lily star, large 5-petal hibiscus) collapse to one model differing only by color, and iconic callas are unrecognizable. At June nearly half the archetype is non-blooming and reduces to indistinguishable bare stems; the bronze-foliage hardy-hibiscus cultivars are a correct standout. No egregious wrong colors.
summaries.group_similarity: Color-cultivar sets are handled correctly: the tropical-hibiscus color variants and the calla color variants are adequately distinguished along the color axis, which is the right axis for same-species color cultivars. The distinctiveness FAILURES are on the form axis: (1) different genera (calla, true lily, amaryllis, hibiscus) all share one generic multi-petal pom, so same-color/different-species plants collide (e.g. yellow calla vs yellow tropical hibiscus vs Hula Girl are identical), and (2) at the captured June month ~22 of 45 plants are non-blooming and collapse to near-identical thin crossed stems. Net: adequately distinct by color, inadequately distinct by shape/form.

summaries.summary: The rosette archetype has only 4 species (all shown top-down at June in a single image); all render as flat, thin-spiked radiating starbursts. The main gap is distinctiveness: every plant shares the same spiky-star silhouette, distinguished only by aloe's (correct) orange bloom stalk and echeveria's pink center. Colors are plausible, but the thin-spike form misrepresents the broad-leaved succulent rosettes (echeveria, hens-and-chicks).
summaries.group_similarity: Distinctiveness is weak. All four models are the same radiating spiky star, so identity rests on accents and size rather than form. Agave and hens-and-chicks are effectively identical (grey/green spiky stars, no accent), which is misleading given how different those plants are in life. Aloe is well differentiated by its orange raceme; echeveria is partly differentiated by its pink center and smaller size. To read as distinct, the soft succulent rosettes (echeveria, hens-and-chicks) need broad, tight, layered leaves versus long swords for agave/aloe, plus color cues (agave glaucous blue-grey, sempervivum red-tipped).

summaries.summary: The panicle archetype (12 bee-balm cultivars, 12 garden-phlox cultivars, Joe Pye Weed) shows a serious distinctiveness gap: at June every plant is an identical green sphere-cluster with zero bloom colour, so the colour-named cultivars that make up almost the entire page are indistinguishable. Secondary issues: the dwarf 'Pardon My' bee-balms render as the largest models (backwards), giant Joe Pye Weed renders undersized, and the bare vertical stems read as wires above the foliage. The overall upright-clump form is otherwise acceptable.
summaries.group_similarity: Too similar. All 26 panicle plants are reduced to near-identical green sphere-clusters at the captured June month, with no bloom colour or flower-head geometry to tell them apart. This is a severe distinctiveness gap because the archetype is overwhelmingly composed of colour-named cultivars (scarlet/purple/pink/white bee-balms; blue/white/orange/crimson phloxes) whose only meaningful visual difference is flower colour. Even the three genera present (Monarda, Phlox paniculata, Eutrochium) share one generic mound-plus-stems shape despite very different real flower-head forms. To be adequately distinct these models need their bloom colour to show at the snapshot month, and ideally differentiated flower-head silhouettes (shaggy whorls vs domed panicles vs large flat-topped joe-pye domes).

summaries.summary: Solid archetype overall: the mound-with-emergent-spikes form is appropriate and June bloom colors are largely accurate across ~70 species (salvias, veronicas, catmints, lavenders, angelonias, baptisias all bloom convincingly, and cross-genus distinction is helped by grey vs dark-green foliage). Two clear defects stand out: liatris (Blazing Star) renders as a tiny bloomless green speck - a major scale and identity outlier - and agastache (Anise Hyssop) shows no bloom in June when it should be a prominent purple spike. The remaining weakness is within-genus distinctiveness among the blue/purple Salvia nemorosa cultivars and blue veronicas.
summaries.group_similarity: Cross-genus distinctiveness is good: grey-green foliage separates catmints and lavenders from the dark-green salvias, veronicas and baptisias, and the bloom palette is genuinely wide (blue, violet, pink, magenta, white, yellow, maroon, and a correct scarlet for salvia-annual). The gap is within-genus: the Salvia nemorosa group (caradonna, may-night, april-night, east-friesland) and the blue veronicas (royal-candles, sunny-border-blue, crater-lake-blue, giles-van-hees) are near-identical, differentiated only by nearly-matching hues rather than the height/stem/spike-density differences that distinguish them in life. Because those cultivars are genuinely similar in reality, this is a polish gap rather than a misleading error.

summaries.summary: The plume archetype is largely well executed: the upright feathery-spike-on-a-foliage-mound form fits astilbe, celosia, goatsbeard, foam flower, goldenrod and queen-of-the-prairie, and bloom timing is handled correctly (amaranth and goldenrod are green in June and correctly color up by September). The main issues are (1) crested cockscomb celosias (Celosia cristata) forced into a feathery-plume silhouette instead of their iconic crest, (2) Foam Flower rendered oversized for a small groundcover while genuinely tall species render small, and (3) two late astilbes (Sprite, Purple Candles) never showing their signature color in the captured months. Colors are accurate wherever a plant is actually in bloom - no wrong-color cases found.
summaries.group_similarity: Adequately distinct WHEN in bloom - the archetype leans almost entirely on flower color to separate cultivars, and this works well for the celosia rainbow and the astilbe color range. The weakness is form uniformity: every plume shares one upright silhouette, so out of bloom the plants collapse to interchangeable green mounds (all 5 amaranths in June; most astilbes by September), and the crested (cristata) celosias are visually merged with the feathery (plumosa) ones despite very different real-life forms. Within-color sub-groups (three deep-red astilbes; several red and several pink celosias) are hard to tell apart, but that is mostly faithful since they are same-species cultivars.

summaries.summary: The showy archetype holds 56 cultivars across dahlia, rose, peony, lisianthus and ranunculus. The peony/rose/lisianthus half is well done - large layered blooms in correct, distinct colors (Bartzella yellow, Coral Charm/Coral Sunset coral, Karl Rosenfield/Red Charm red, Mister Lincoln deep red, Duchesse de Nemours/Festiva Maxima white, all lisianthus colors). The core problem is timing: at the only captured month (June) all 16 dahlias and all 11 ranunculus are out of bloom and collapse into near-identical green foliage blobs, leaving roughly half the archetype undifferentiated.
summaries.group_similarity: Mixed. The blooming groups (peony ~20, rose ~5, lisianthus ~8) are adequately distinct - varied, botanically-correct bloom colors make each cultivar readable at June. The dahlia (16) and ranunculus (11) groups are NOT distinct at the captured month: with no June bloom they are indistinguishable green clumps (dahlias get a little variety only from four dark-foliage cultivars). Both groups would need a bloom-month capture plus per-cultivar bloom color to be told apart.

summaries.summary: The umbel archetype (30 species: 6 Pentas, ~9 Sedum, ~12 Yarrow cultivars, plus butterfly-weed, dill, lady's-mantle) is mostly solid - Pentas colors and warm-toned yarrows read distinctly in June, sedums correctly stay green in June and bloom in September, and purple-emperor's dark foliage is a nice distinctive touch. Main issues: dill is nearly invisible (scale/legibility), base 'yarrow' is colored yellow instead of white, the bright fall sedums cluster into one indistinct magenta mass in September, and all yarrows drop to generic green blobs by September. No side views were available for this archetype, so form was not deeply assessed.
summaries.group_similarity: Distinctiveness is adequate in bloom but uneven. The three large cultivar families are differentiated primarily by flower color, which is appropriate. Pentas (6 colors) separate cleanly. Yarrow cultivars read well in June but become an indistinguishable wall of green in September since color is their only differentiator. Sedums are the strongest in September but the bright ones (neon/brilliant/mr-goodbud/thunderhead/autumn-fire) crowd into one magenta-pink cluster; the paler ones (frosty-morn, matrona, autumn) and dark-foliage purple-emperor stand apart. Overall: distinct enough at peak bloom for each family, but with a real September gap for yarrows and a bright-sedum overlap.

summaries.summary: This page is 12 sunflower cultivars rendered at June; all appear as generic green leafy stalks with no flower heads, so cultivars distinguished only by bloom color are indistinguishable, and no bloom-month images were supplied to verify colors. Height also fails to track cultivar: the dwarf 'Teddy Bear' renders as the largest model while the giant 'Mammoth' renders small - an inversion of reality.
summaries.group_similarity: Poor distinctiveness. All 12 species are one species (Helianthus annuus) whose cultivar differentiation is almost entirely bloom color plus, for two, height. At the captured month (June) no blooms are shown, so the models are near-indistinguishable green leafy stalks. Even with blooms, the cream trio (italian-white, vanilla-ice, lemon-queen) and the dark-red pair (moulin-rouge, velvet-queen) would remain very similar and would rely on color to separate. The one differentiator that could work without blooms - height (giant Mammoth vs dwarf Teddy Bear) - is not only unused but inverted.

summaries.summary: The shrub archetype is generally strong: June-blooming families (roses, butterfly bush, potentilla, spirea, ninebark, rhododendron, gardenia) show accurate, well-differentiated bloom and foliage colors. The main gaps are (1) a large no-bloom-generic distinctiveness problem for out-of-season families at June (Rose of Sharon, lilac, camellia, crape myrtle, summersweet, viburnum, winterberry, witch hazel all reduce to identical green mounds), (2) two columnar cultivars (boxwood-graham-blandy, rose-of-sharon-purple-pillar) rendered as tiny specks that look broken, and (3) five dark-purple smokebush cultivars that are near-indistinguishable.
summaries.group_similarity: Mixed. The June-blooming families are handled very well and are clearly distinct: roses/David Austin cultivars (accurate per-cultivar bloom colors), butterfly bush (correct gray-green foliage + differentiated flower colors), potentilla, spirea (golden vs green foliage cultivars), ninebark (excellent burgundy/copper/gold foliage range), rhododendron, gardenia, mock orange, and hydrangea all read as different plants. The weakness is the many out-of-bloom families at June - Rose of Sharon (13 cultivars), lilacs, camellias, crape myrtles, summersweet, most viburnum, winterberry, witch hazel - which collapse into interchangeable green mounds. Within those groups distinctiveness is essentially zero at this month, though it is seasonally honest. One cross-cultivar over-similarity that is NOT seasonal: the five dark-purple smokebush cultivars.

summaries.summary: The 'tree' archetype (82 spring-flowering ornamentals, Japanese maples, and a few shade trees) is modeled as one rounded blob canopy differentiated only by color and size. Color assignments are largely accurate and thoughtful, but at the captured June month most plants have finished blooming and reduce to near-identical green blobs, and no form variation distinguishes weeping, columnar, or giant shade trees. Main issues are a distinctiveness gap (too-similar cherries, magnolias, and red Japanese maples), a uniform form that ignores weeping/columnar habits, and undersized large shade trees (tulip-tree, linden).
summaries.group_similarity: Plants in this archetype are too similar. Every one of the ~82 species uses a single rounded cluster-of-spheres canopy form, so differentiation depends entirely on foliage color and size. Color is used well (Japanese maples red, purple-leaf redbuds/crabapples purple, chartreuse cultivars yellow-green, variegated Wolf Eyes pale, and Kousa/Southern/Sweetbay/Satomi carrying flower-dots), but because the archetype is dominated by spring-flowering ornamentals that share plain green summer foliage, the June capture leaves large indistinguishable clusters: ~10 flowering cherries, ~10 magnolias, ~4 serviceberries, and several dogwoods each look interchangeable. To read as distinct they need form differentiation (weeping, columnar, pyramidal, and honest relative size for big shade trees) and a stronger, more persistent bloom than the few tiny April dots currently shown.

summaries.summary: The vase-shrub archetype is dominated by weigela cultivars (well-differentiated by pink/red/white flowers and green/burgundy/cream foliage) plus forsythia, spirea, and beautyberry groups. No wrong-model or unrecognizable cases. The real weaknesses are distinctiveness gaps at the June capture: the six green forsythias are indistinguishable, and the four green beautyberries collapse together because their identity (fall berry color, incl. white for beautyberry-white) is invisible in June. One off color: beautyberry-american is rendered lime/chartreuse rather than green. Form reads as a squat mound more than an upright arching vase.
summaries.group_similarity: Mixed. Strong point: weigela cultivars are genuinely and appropriately distinguished by flower color (pink vs red vs white) and foliage (green vs burgundy vs pale-variegated), which mirrors how the real cultivars actually differ - this is good design. Weak points: (1) the six green forsythias (forsythia, forsythia-gold-tide, forsythia-lynwood-gold, forsythia-magical-gold, forsythia-northern-gold, forsythia-show-off) are one identical blob at June; forsythia-fiesta is the only distinct one. (2) The four green beautyberries (beautyberry, beautyberry-early-amethyst, beautyberry-profusion, beautyberry-white) are indistinguishable because their differentiator is fall berry color, not visible at month 6. (3) A few weigela pairs are visual duplicates (spilled-wine/wine-and-roses burgundy; my-monet/variegata cream; white/white-knight green+white). Both distinctiveness gaps would largely resolve with a spring capture for forsythia and a fall capture for beautyberry, which the current June-only set cannot show.

summaries.summary: Overall a high-quality batch of 240 professional cutouts across sheets 26-30; the vast majority correctly match their slugs with clean edges and no duplicates or kingdom mismatches detected. The only issues are a handful of soft color mismatches, concentrated in the red-ranunculus run (salmon/magenta reading as plain red) and rex-begonia-burgundy (plain green), plus a green Tamukeyama maple that should be red.
summaries.group_similarity: Adjacent same-genus cultivars are mostly well differentiated by color (rose-of-sharon, rhododendron, sedum, snapdragon, stock, strawflower, statice, sweet-pea, sweet-alyssum, sunflower, spirea runs all read as distinct). Two genuinely too-similar clusters: (1) the blue/purple salvia run on sheet 27 (salvia-april-night, blue-hill, blue-marvel, caradonna, east-friesland, may-night) is effectively indistinguishable cultivar-to-cultivar, though these plants really are near-identical in life; (2) the red-ranunculus cluster on sheet 26 (magenta, red, salmon, and base ranunculus) all render as very similar reds when magenta and salmon should diverge. The Cotinus smokebush cluster on sheet 28 (royal-purple, velvet-cloak, winecraft-black, young-lady) is borderline but shows enough tonal variation to pass.

summaries.summary: Across sheets 6-10 (~240 plants, butterfly-bush through crabapple) the cutouts are overwhelmingly correct professional cut-outs with clean edges and good per-cultivar color distinction. The one clear issue is calla-lily-black, which shows a white calla lily (wrong color, and a near-duplicate of the plain white calla). A handful of foliage cultivars (coleus-redhead, coral-bells-berry-smoothie, coral-bells-plum-pudding) and coneflower-green-jewel look like they may carry the wrong color, but these are low-confidence at contact-sheet resolution and would benefit from a higher-res check.
summaries.group_similarity: Genus runs are generally well differentiated by flower color: butterfly-bush, calibrachoa, calla-lily (except the black one), chrysanthemum, clematis, coneflower/echinacea and cosmos all show visibly distinct cultivar colors, so cultivars are distinguishable. Catmint cultivars (sheet 7 row 5) are near-identical blue-purple mounds, but that reflects the real plants (botanically expected, not a defect); only catmint-snowflake (white) and catmint-pink-cat stand apart. The coleus and coral-bells (heuchera) foliage runs are the hardest to tell apart, and a few of those cells appear to carry the wrong foliage color (see findings) rather than being true duplicates. No exact photo duplicates or broken/empty/pixelated cutouts were spotted across the five sheets.

summaries.summary: Across sheets 16-20 the cut-outs are high-quality professional images and the vast majority correctly match their slugs, with strong color differentiation within most cultivar runs. Two genuine species mismatches stand out (glory-of-the-snow-pink and golden-raindrops), plus a generic ginkgo and a couple of cultivars that fail to show their defining characteristic (japanese-painted-fern, japanese-forest-grass-red).
summaries.group_similarity: Genus runs are generally well-differentiated by color: gladiolus, hyacinth, larkspur, lilac, impatiens, lantana, gomphrena, and hellebore cultivars are each distinguishable. Hostas (sheet 18) look broadly similar but carry genuine variegation/color differences that hold up. Two weak runs: the standard purple lavender cultivars on sheet 20 (french/grosso/hidcote/munstead/phenomenal/provence/royal-velvet/spanish) are essentially indistinguishable purple mounds, and the japanese-forest-grass 'red' + japanese-painted-fern greens do not show cultivar-specific coloring so they blur into their plain-green siblings.

summaries.summary: These five cutout sheets are mostly clean professional cut-outs with correct color/species matching. The one real, systematic issue is three red-foliage Japanese maple cultivars (crimson-queen, emperor, fireglow) all rendered with green foliage, defeating the whole reason a user would pick them; a fourth cutout (crape-myrtle-black-diamond-red) similarly loses its signature dark foliage. No wrong-species, no animal/object, and no exact duplicates found; two cultivar runs (forsythias, gardenias) are weakly distinguishable but reflect real-world similarity.
summaries.group_similarity: Most cultivar runs are adequately distinct and correctly colored - the dahlias (sheet 12), delphiniums (sheets 12-13), dianthus (sheet 13), foxgloves (sheet 14), gaillardia and gazania (sheet 15), and creeping-phlox (sheet 11) all show appropriate color variation between cultivars. Two runs are weak: the forsythias (sheet 14, all yellow, three near-identical) and the gardenias (sheet 15, all white-on-green, several interchangeable), though both reflect real-world similarity within those genera. Expected-similar runs that are acceptable: the red crape myrtles (cherry-dazzle/dynamite/red-rocket, sheet 11), the yellow daylilies (going-bananas/happy-returns/hyperion/stella-de-oro, sheet 12), and the blue delphiniums (blue-bird/blue-jay/blue-mirror, sheet 12).

summaries.summary: test

Cutout quality across sheets 31-34 is high and largely accurate. The two clearest issues are toad-lily (an airy pink mound that is not Tricyrtis) and tulip-spring-green (shown solid yellow instead of the cultivar's white-and-green). Minor issues: a visible pot in vinca-apricot, the tropical-hibiscus series lacking characteristic large blooms, two parrot tulips shown in plain (non-parrot) form, zinnia-envy-green not showing its signature lime green, and a couple of same-colour cultivar runs (pink veronicas, yellow yarrows) that are hard to tell apart.
summaries.group_similarity: Overall these four sheets are well differentiated, mostly because cultivars are separated by flower colour (tulips, verbena, weigela, wisteria, witch-hazel, zinnia, and most yarrows all read as distinct). The genuine too-similar runs are same-colour cultivar groups: the pink/magenta Veronica spicata spikes (first-love / giles-van-hees / pink-damask / red-fox) and the yellow yarrows (coronation-gold / moonshine / sunny-seduction / plain). These are hard to distinguish but reflect real cultivar likeness rather than lazy reuse. No confirmed exact-duplicate photos were found.
